messages.content: You are an IICRC S500:2025 certified water damage restoration specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections: 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism, 2. Initial Emergency Services, 3. Affected Materials & Extent, 4. Psychrometric Conditions, 5. Drying Equipment Setup, 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol, 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off. All equipment quantities must cite IICRC S500:2025 ratios. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Generate a scope for a Category 1 Class 2 water damage event. Property: single-storey brick veneer, 3br/1ba, 140 m². Affected area: kitchen and laundry, 28 m². Cause: flexi-hose burst under laundry tub, approximately 5 hours undetected.

## 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism

**Source confirmed:** Laundry tub DN15 braided stainless flexi-hose failed at the isolation valve thread connection. Category 1 clean water (IICRC S500:2025 §4.2 / §5.2). Source isolated at inline isolator — confirmed by attending plumber prior to restoration commencement. Water released under mains pressure (estimated 350–500 kPa) for approximately 5 hours. Loss pathway: laundry tub base → vinyl plank flooring → kitchen doorway threshold → kitchen vinyl tile floor → lower wall cavity (laundry only — moisture detected at 200 mm via probe). No sewage, grey-water, or chemical contamination detected.

## 2. Initial Emergency Services

- Wet vacuum extraction: laundry 10 m², kitchen 18 m² — approximately 190 L recovered (§8.2)
- Content protection: washing machine and dryer isolated and shifted to garage; kitchen base cabinets emptied
- Moisture survey: 14 reading points mapped (§10.1) — maximum reading 74% WME laundry wall lining
- Laundry wall cavity: moisture probe confirms intrusion to 200 mm height — flood cut at 300 mm required
- Documentation: 22 photographs; moisture map sketch completed

## 3. Affected Materials & Extent

**Laundry (10 m²)**
- Vinyl plank flooring (click-lock): surface moisture 68% WME — removal required (water under locking system)
- Plasterboard wall lining: lower 200 mm wet, 74% WME — flood cut at 300 mm
- Particleboard cabinet carcass (1 × laundry unit): base swollen — assess; if delaminated, remove

**Kitchen (18 m²)**
- Vinyl tile flooring (adhesive-set): moisture ingress under tile adhesive, 42% WME — remove for subfloor drying access
- Concrete slab below: 16% WME — dry in place
- Plasterboard lower 50 mm: minimal moisture, 22% WME — monitor, no immediate removal

Material classifications per IICRC S500:2025 §6: vinyl plank = semi-porous; particleboard = porous; plasterboard = porous; concrete = non-porous.

## 4. Psychrometric Conditions

Baseline (Day 1, 08:45):
- Temperature: 26.8°C / RH: 65% / GPP: 137 / Dew point: 19.8°C
- Reference outdoor: 25°C / 68% RH

Drying targets (S500:2025 §11.4): plasterboard ≤17% WME; concrete slab ≤4% WME; timber subfloor (if present) ≤16% WME.

## 5. Drying Equipment Setup

Affected area: 28 m².

| Equipment | Qty | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier (70 L/day) | 1 | 1 per 40 m² — S500:2025 |
| Air movers (axial 1.5 kW) | 3 | 1 per 15 m² + 1 × flood-cut wall cavity |
| HEPA Air Scrubber | Not required | Category 1 — no biological contamination |

Air mover placement: 2 × floor level (45° to wet surfaces); 1 × directed through flood-cut opening. Dehumidifier drain hose to laundry drain.

Electrical load: 3 × 1.5 kW + 1 × 0.8 kW = 5.3 kW — compliant with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (≤80% of 10 A GPO circuit).

## 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol

Monitoring: 09:00 daily. Moisture readings: laundry wall lining (3 points), kitchen slab (4 points), flood-cut plasterboard edges (2 points). Psychrometric log: temperature, RH, GPP per visit. Dehumidifier water tank check daily. Air mover position confirmed. Photographs: minimum 4 per visit. Equipment electrical check at each visit.

## 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off

Drying goal achieved when: plasterboard ≤17% WME, concrete slab ≤4% WME — 3 consecutive compliant readings. Estimated duration: **5–6 days** (Class 2, Category 1). Drying Goal Certificate issued in RestoreAssist (§11.4). Reinstatement trigger: flooring replacement, plasterboard patch at flood cut, painting. Plumber to fit new flexi-hose on reinstatement.

You are an IICRC S500:2025 certified water damage restoration specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections: 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism, 2. Initial Emergency Services, 3. Affected Materials & Extent, 4. Psychrometric Conditions, 5. Drying Equipment Setup, 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol, 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off. All equipment quantities must cite IICRC S500:2025 ratios. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Generate a scope for a Category 2 Class 2 water damage event. Property: 1970s brick veneer, 3br/1ba, 120 m². Cause: dishwasher door seal failure, warm soapy water, kitchen and adjacent dining area affected, 24 m².

## 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism

**Source confirmed:** Dishwasher door seal perished — Category 2 classification (IICRC S500:2025 §5.3) due to warm water (55°C cycle) containing detergent and food-particle contamination. Source isolated at dishwasher isolator valve. Loss pathway: kitchen floor → dishwasher kickplate area → vinyl tile floor → dining room carpet.

## 2. Initial Emergency Services

- Wet vacuum extraction: kitchen 10 m², dining 14 m² — approximately 120 L recovered
- Category 2 antimicrobial application: EPA-registered broad-spectrum antimicrobial to all wet porous surfaces post-extraction (§5.3)
- Dining carpet and underlay: Category 2 contamination, fully saturated — removal required; disposal
- Content protection: dining table and chairs shifted to living room
- 18 photographs; moisture map completed

## 3. Affected Materials & Extent

**Kitchen (10 m²)**
- Vinyl tile floor (adhesive-set): moisture 48% WME — removal required for slab drying
- Concrete slab: 20% WME — dry in place with antimicrobial treatment
- Plasterboard kickspace (lower 100 mm): 34% WME — monitor; flood cut at 200 mm if elevated Day 3

**Dining (14 m²)**
- Carpet + underlay: Category 2 — removed and disposed
- Concrete slab: 22% WME — dry in place post-antimicrobial

All porous materials in Category 2 water contact treated with antimicrobial per §5.3.

## 4. Psychrometric Conditions

Baseline: Temp 27.2°C / RH 69% / GPP 146 / Dew point 21.0°C. Ambient subtropical QLD — dehumidification essential. Targets: slab ≤4% WME; plasterboard ≤17% WME (§11.4).

## 5. Drying Equipment Setup

Affected area: 24 m².

| Equipment | Qty | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier (70 L/day) | 1 | 1 per 40 m² |
| Air movers (axial 1.5 kW) | 2 | 1 per 15 m² (24 ÷ 15 = 1.6 → 2) |
| HEPA Air Scrubber (600 m³/h) | 1 | Mandatory Category 2 — §5.3 |

## 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol

Monitoring daily 09:00. Slab readings (6 points), plasterboard lower kick space (3 points). Antimicrobial re-application Day 3 if RH >60%. HEPA scrubber filter inspection every 48 hours.

## 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off

All slab readings ≤4% WME; plasterboard ≤17% WME; indoor RH ≤50%; 3 consecutive readings compliant. Estimated: **5–7 days** (Class 2, Category 2). Drying Goal Certificate issued. Antimicrobial treatment certificate provided to insurer. Reinstatement: slab seal, new vinyl, carpet replacement, dishwasher seal replaced by appliance technician.

You are an IICRC S500:2025 certified water damage restoration specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections: 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism, 2. Initial Emergency Services, 3. Affected Materials & Extent, 4. Psychrometric Conditions, 5. Drying Equipment Setup, 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol, 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off. All equipment quantities must cite IICRC S500:2025 ratios. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Scope for a Category 3 Class 3 water damage. 4br/2ba brick veneer, 210 m². Entire ground floor 80 m² affected by sewage backup from blocked council main. Sewage inundation to 150 mm depth in living areas. Hardwood flooring ground floor.

## 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism

**Source confirmed:** Council sewer main blockage (tree root intrusion — council CCTV confirmation attached). Sewage backflow through ground-floor wet areas and sub-floor drain point. Category 3 black water (IICRC S500:2025 §5.4) — raw sewage contamination. All occupants vacated. Health advisory issued in writing. Council notified — liability investigation ongoing. **All porous structural materials with Category 3 water contact must be removed — no exceptions (§5.4).** Source rectified by council crew, drain cleared.

## 2. Initial Emergency Services

- Level B PPE deployed: full-face respirator (P3/OV combination), impermeable Tyvek coveralls, double nitrile gloves, boot covers
- Sewage extraction: 80 m² ground floor, 150 mm depth — approximately 12,000 L via truck-mounted extraction unit
- Hardwood flooring (80 m²): Category 3 contact — removal commenced; waste classified as infectious
- Carpet and underlay (if present): none — hardwood throughout
- 65 photographs under full PPE; entry/exit log

## 3. Affected Materials & Extent

**Ground floor (80 m²) — all Category 3 contact:**
- Hardwood T&G flooring (spotted gum, 80×19 mm): fully saturated, Cat 3 — remove and dispose as infectious waste
- Timber bearers and joists: sewage saturation — remove affected sections; retain structurally sound sections if moisture ≤30% WME post-decontamination
- Plasterboard lower 600 mm (all perimeter walls): Cat 3 contact — flood cut at 600 mm, dispose
- Insulation within wall cavities (if present): remove and dispose
- Concrete slab: 32% WME, sewage residue — decontaminate with EPA-registered broad-spectrum antimicrobial × 3 applications

## 4. Psychrometric Conditions

Baseline (post-extraction, Day 1): Temp 25.6°C / RH 81% / GPP 172 / Dew point 22.1°C. Extended drying time expected due to high ambient RH and concrete subfloor mass. Category 3 protocol requires continuous HEPA air scrubbing during active decontamination.

## 5. Drying Equipment Setup

Affected area: 80 m².

| Equipment | Qty | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier (100 L/day) | 2 | 1 per 40 m² |
| Air movers (axial 1.5 kW) | 8 | 1 per 15 m² = 5.3 + 3 × flood-cut wall cavities |
| HEPA Air Scrubber (800 m³/h) | 2 | Mandatory Cat 3; continuous operation §5.4 |

All equipment surfaces decontaminated with antimicrobial before removal from site. Antimicrobial: 3 × applications to all exposed structural surfaces on Days 1, 2, and 4.

## 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol

Twice-daily monitoring Days 1–3; daily thereafter. 24 reading points: concrete slab, timber framing, plasterboard remnants. Odour assessment each visit — persistent odour indicates incomplete decontamination. ATP swab test of concrete slab at Day 5 — trigger for hygienist clearance.

## 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off

Mandatory independent hygienist ATP clearance test before any reinstatement (Cat 3). All concrete slab readings ≤4% WME; timber framing ≤16% WME; 3 consecutive compliant readings. Estimated duration: **10–14 days** (Class 3, Category 3). Drying Goal Certificate issued. Cat 3 remediation certificate + antimicrobial warranty to insurer. Council liability documentation filed. Reinstatement: licensed builder — new hardwood flooring or alternative, plasterboard replacement, painting.

You are an IICRC S500:2025 certified water damage restoration specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections. All equipment quantities must cite IICRC S500:2025 ratios. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Scope for a Category 1 Class 4 event. Single-storey Queenslander, 3br/1ba, 155 m². Living and dining room hardwood T&G flooring 40 m² affected by roof leak during storm. Roof now tarped. No ceiling insulation in this section.

## 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism

**Source confirmed:** Roof tile displacement during storm event — Category 1 (clean rainwater, §5.2). Source secured: temporary tarpaulin installed by roofing contractor. Water pathway: displaced tile → exposed sarking → battens → hardwood T&G ceiling lining → air gap → hardwood T&G floor. No contamination detected. Class 4 determination: hardwood flooring (low permeance, high density) — specialty drying protocol applies per S500:2025 §7.5.

## 2. Initial Emergency Services

- Extraction: standing water removed, approximately 40 L from floor surface
- Roof space inspection: battens showing moisture, sarking torn (1.2 m²), no insulation present in this bay — direct exposure to hardwood ceiling lining
- Hardwood ceiling boards: moisture 48% WME — monitor; no removal unless structural compromise
- Hardwood floor boards: 54–68% WME — Class 4 drying protocol commenced
- 28 photographs; moisture grid established (10 points)

## 3. Affected Materials & Extent

**Living and dining (40 m²)**
- Hardwood T&G floor (80×19 mm, estimated age 80 years, hardwood species unconfirmed): 54–68% WME — Class 4, dry in place
- Hardwood T&G ceiling boards: 48% WME — dry in place; structural integrity confirmed
- Timber battens (roof space): 52% WME — directed airflow to sub-roof via ceiling access hatch
- No carpet, no underlay, no plasterboard in scope

Class 4 per §7.5: very low permeance material (hardwood, >30% initial MC) requiring extended drying cycle beyond standard protocols.

## 4. Psychrometric Conditions

Baseline: Temp 28.0°C / RH 74% / GPP 158 / Dew point 23.2°C. Elevated ambient RH will significantly impede hardwood drying. QLD summer — desiccant dehumidification standby if Day 7 average reading >30% WME.

## 5. Drying Equipment Setup

| Equipment | Qty | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier (100 L/day) | 1 | 1 per 40 m² |
| Air movers (centrifugal low-profile) | 4 | Low-profile placement flat on hardwood for Class 4; 1 per 15 m² = 2.7 → 4 for Class 4 uplift |
| HEPA Scrubber | Not required | Category 1 |

Air mover strategy: centrifugal low-profile units placed flat at 45° to board grain — maximises surface air velocity across hardwood. Open roof access hatch — 1 × air mover directed at batten zone for ceiling/roof drying.

## 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol

Monitoring daily. Hardwood floor grid: 10 points. Ceiling board: 4 points. Roof batten: 2 points. Cupping measurement weekly (straight-edge across 3 boards, gap ≤2 mm acceptable). Psychrometric log. Desiccant escalation review at Day 7.

## 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off

Hardwood EMC target: ≤14% WME (QLD stable EMC for interior hardwood per §11.4, climate-adjusted). 3 consecutive daily readings across all 10 floor points. Cupping: if residual cupping >2 mm at validation — engage flooring specialist for light sand and re-coat assessment. Estimated duration: **14–21 days** (Class 4). Drying Goal Certificate issued. Reinstatement: tile replacement by licensed roofer, sarking patch, interior painting only if ceiling boards show staining.

You are an IICRC S500:2025 certified water damage restoration specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Short scope for a Category 1 Class 1 event. Granny flat, 1br/1ba, 42 m². Bathroom basin overflow, under-bench tap left running. 5 m² bathroom affected. No subfloor moisture. Category 1, 30-minute event.

## 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism

Basin cold-water tap left running — overflow via basin waste into bathroom floor. Category 1 clean water (§5.2). Source isolated. 30-minute event — minimal saturation. Class 1: only part of room affected, low-porosity materials. §7.2.

## 2. Initial Emergency Services

Wet vacuum: 5 m² bathroom floor — approximately 8 L removed. Contents dry. No structural penetration. Ceramic tile floor confirmed — no subfloor moisture detected via probe.

## 3. Affected Materials & Extent

**Bathroom (5 m²)**
- Ceramic tile floor: non-porous, wet surface — dry in place, no removal
- Plasterboard lower 80 mm (vanity wall): 26% WME — monitor; target ≤17% WME
- No carpet, no subfloor involvement

Class 1: limited affected area, non-porous primary material. §7.2.

## 4. Psychrometric Conditions

Baseline: Temp 24.5°C / RH 60% / GPP 122 / Dew point 16.5°C. Favourable drying conditions.

## 5. Drying Equipment Setup

| Equipment | Qty | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier (30 L/day) | 1 | Minimum 1 unit for any Class 1 event |
| Air mover (1.5 kW) | 1 | 1 unit for 5 m² |

## 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol

Check every 48 hours. Plasterboard 3 points — target ≤17% WME. Ceramic tile surface check. Psychrometric log.

## 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off

Plasterboard ≤17% WME. Estimated: **2–3 days** (Class 1, Category 1). Drying Goal Certificate issued. No reinstatement required — cosmetic check of grout only.

You are an IICRC S500:2025 certified water damage restoration specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Scope for a multi-unit Category 2 Class 3 event. Upstairs apartment 3A (washing machine overflow) caused water damage to unit 2A directly below. Affected: unit 3A laundry 8 m², unit 2A bedroom and living room ceiling and walls, 38 m². Body corporate property.

## 1. Water Source & Loss Mechanism

Unit 3A washing machine supply hose failed at machine inlet — Category 2 grey water (hot water + detergent, §5.3). Source isolated at laundry isolation valve. Water migrated through concrete intermediate slab penetrations (conduit rough-ins) into Unit 2A ceiling void. Loss pathway: Unit 3A floor → slab penetration → Unit 2A ceiling void → plasterboard ceiling → wall cavities to 600 mm. Body Corporate notified; both unit owners/tenants vacated Unit 2A.

## 2. Initial Emergency Services

- Unit 3A: extraction 8 m² — approx 30 L; antimicrobial treatment (Cat 2, §5.3)
- Unit 2A: emergency ceiling access — 4 × inspection holes drilled; standing water confirmed in ceiling void
- Ceiling void extraction via industrial wet vacuum — approximately 90 L
- Unit 2A carpet (living 20 m²): Cat 2 contamination via ceiling drip — remove and dispose
- Occupants of Unit 2A relocated; body corporate notified of expected 10-day disruption
- 46 photographs; two-property moisture map

## 3. Affected Materials & Extent

**Unit 3A (8 m²):** Vinyl plank floor — Cat 2 contact, removed. Concrete slab 28% WME.
**Unit 2A (38 m²):** Plasterboard ceiling 10 mm (38 m²) — buckled, Cat 2 contact, remove. Wall lining lower 600 mm (4 × walls) — flood cut. Carpet + underlay removed. Intermediate concrete slab: 24% WME — dry in place + antimicrobial.

## 4. Psychrometric Conditions

Unit 2A baseline: Temp 26.4°C / RH 72% / GPP 153 / Dew point 21.3°C.

## 5. Drying Equipment Setup

Unit 2A affected area: 38 m².

| Equipment | Qty | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier (100 L/day) | 1 | 1 per 40 m² |
| Air movers (axial 1.5 kW) | 6 | 1 per 15 m² = 2.5 + 4 flood-cut wall panels |
| HEPA Air Scrubber | 1 | Mandatory Cat 2, §5.3 |

Unit 3A: 1 × air mover + 1 × dehumidifier (slab drying). Two separate drying systems — cross-tenant billing documented.

## 6. Daily Monitoring Protocol

Daily monitoring both units. Unit 2A: 18 reading points (slab 6, wall framing 8, plasterboard 4). Cross-contamination check between units at each visit. Body corporate daily report issued.

## 7. Drying Validation & Sign-Off

All slab ≤4% WME; framing ≤16% WME; plasterboard ≤17% WME. Estimated: **8–10 days** (Class 3, Category 2). Separate Drying Goal Certificates for Unit 3A and Unit 2A. Body Corporate insurers (may be separate policies) — both certificates required. Reinstatement: ceiling replacement Unit 2A, carpet, wall lining, painting. Plumber to assess and repair unit 3A washing machine connection.

You are an IICRC S520 certified mould remediation specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections: 1. Visual Assessment & Contamination Mapping, 2. Moisture Source Identification, 3. Contamination Boundary, 4. Containment Setup, 5. Remediation Scope, 6. Disposal Protocol, 7. Clearance Testing Recommendation. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Scope for a Condition 3 mould remediation. 1970s brick veneer, 3br/1ba, 130 m². Mould found in bathroom wall cavity and adjacent hallway. Visible growth 2.4 m² plasterboard. Cause: perished shower silicone — chronic water ingress over 2+ years. Independent hygienist pre-assessment report available.

## 1. Visual Assessment & Contamination Mapping

Pre-assessment hygienist report (reference: [hygienist report no.]) confirms:

- **Bathroom (5 m²):** Condition 3. Visible mould growth 2.4 m² on plasterboard behind shower wall (tile removed for inspection). Multiple species confirmed by hygienist: Stachybotrys chartarum, Cladosporium spp. Moisture: plasterboard 94% WME, timber stud 56% WME. Photos 1–22.
- **Hallway adjacent (3 m²):** Condition 2. Settled spore contamination (hygienist air sample elevated 4× outdoor reference). No visible growth. Moisture 44% WME lower wall. Photos 23–30.
- **Bedrooms:** Condition 1 — within normal range per hygienist air sample. No remediation required.

## 2. Moisture Source Identification

Per IICRC S520 §5.1 — source must be rectified before remediation:

1. Shower silicone: perished at all 4 corners and bath/tile junction — water confirmed ingressing during flood test. **Licensed tiler required to strip and re-silicone entire shower before remediation.** Written trade confirmation required.
2. No additional sources identified. Exhaust fan functional — adequate ventilation confirmed.

Remediation scope is conditional on receipt of tiler's completion confirmation and 48-hour cure period for new silicone.

## 3. Contamination Boundary

Per IICRC S520 §6.3 — 500 mm mandatory clearance beyond visible growth:

- Bathroom visible growth 2.4 m² + 500 mm perimeter → remediation zone: **5.8 m² plasterboard**
- Hallway Condition 2 zone: lower 600 mm entire hallway wall → **2.8 m² plasterboard**
- Total plasterboard removal: 8.6 m²
- Timber framing within boundary: assess on opening; noggings and bottom plate at highest risk

## 4. Containment Setup

**Bathroom — Condition 3, critical barrier (§7):**
- 200 µm poly floor-to-ceiling seal, duct-taped to adjacent surfaces
- Slit entry (300 mm overlap)
- HEPA air scrubber 600 m³/h: verify ≥12.5 Pa negative pressure differential via digital manometer before work commences
- PPE: full-face respirator P3, Tyvek Level B coveralls, double nitrile, boot covers

**Hallway — Condition 2:**
- Precautionary sheeting around work area
- P2 respirator, nitrile gloves, disposable coveralls

## 5. Remediation Scope

**Bathroom:**
- Remove all plasterboard within 5.8 m² boundary
- HEPA vacuum all exposed framing and surfaces
- Wet wipe EPA-registered antimicrobial (Benefect Botanical or equivalent) × 2 applications
- Inspect timber framing: bottom plate moisture 56% WME — treat with borate solution (TimBor or equivalent) if structurally sound; replace if unsound
- Second HEPA vacuum after antimicrobial application

**Hallway:**
- Remove plasterboard 2.8 m² boundary zone
- Wet wipe with EPA-registered antimicrobial
- HEPA vacuum

All work per IICRC S520 §8.

## 6. Disposal Protocol

Per §8.4: all removed plasterboard and contaminated material double-bagged in 200 µm poly, sealed and labelled 'Biological Waste — Mould Contaminated Building Material'. Bags removed via dedicated exit (laundry door to side yard — not through habited living areas). Council general waste — QLD local council guidelines confirm non-infectious classification for building material mould waste. Same-day removal.

## 7. Clearance Testing Recommendation

Per §9: independent hygienist clearance mandatory (Condition 3 confirmed, bathroom):
- Spore trap air samples: bathroom (post-remediation, containment removed) + outdoor reference + hallway
- Criteria: Condition 1 restored; no Stachybotrys or elevated Cladosporium in treated zones
- Hygienist must be independent (not affiliated with remediation contractor)
- Reinstatement (tiling, plasterboard, painting) to commence only after written clearance certificate received
- Estimated remediation + clearance timeline: 3–4 days from tiler sign-off

You are an IICRC S520 certified mould remediation specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 7 sections. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Scope for a Condition 2 mould remediation. Rental property, 2br/1ba brick veneer, 90 m². Bathroom ceiling and upper walls show visible mould growth (1.1 m²). Exhaust fan non-functional. Tenant has asthma. Owner engaged restoration company on property manager advice.

## 1. Visual Assessment & Contamination Mapping

- **Bathroom (5 m²):** Condition 2. Visible mould on plasterboard ceiling and upper wall (1.1 m²). Black and green surface mould — likely Cladosporium spp. (surface contamination consistent with condensation-driven growth). Photos 1–14.
- **Adjacent bedroom (1 m²):** Condition 1 — trace mould on window reveal (condensation). No remediation required; cleaning with EPA antimicrobial by owner.
- Hygienist air sample not completed — Condition 2 does not mandate pre-assessment. Visual assessment sufficient per IICRC S520 §4.2.

## 2. Moisture Source Identification

Per §5.1:
1. **Exhaust fan:** non-functional (impeller seized, no airflow). **Electrician required to replace exhaust fan before remediation.** Without this, Condition 2 will recur within 2–3 months.
2. Shower and bath silicone: intact and sealed. No ingress detected. No plumber action required.
3. Confirm exhaust fan replacement in writing before remediation proceeds.

## 3. Contamination Boundary

Visible growth 1.1 m² + 500 mm perimeter → remediation zone: **3.6 m² plasterboard** (ceiling portion + upper wall section). Per §6.3. Boundary does not extend to adjacent bedroom — Condition 1 confirmed.

## 4. Containment Setup

Condition 2 — precautionary containment per §7:
- 200 µm poly sheeting around bathroom work area and doorway
- P2/N95 respirator, nitrile gloves, disposable coveralls, eye protection
- Note: full critical barrier not required for Condition 2 under §7 — precautionary measures sufficient given 1.1 m² affected area
- Tenant with asthma: vacate bathroom and immediately adjacent bedroom during remediation works

## 5. Remediation Scope

- Remove plasterboard within 3.6 m² boundary zone
- HEPA vacuum all exposed surfaces
- Wet wipe EPA-registered antimicrobial × 2 applications on exposed framing
- Inspect framing on opening: if moisture >30% WME → refer to water damage drying protocol before mould scope proceeds
- Per §8

## 6. Disposal Protocol

Per §8.4: removed plasterboard double-bagged in 200 µm poly, labelled, removed same day. Council general waste stream — QLD classification for mould-contaminated building material. Volume: approximately 0.2 m³.

## 7. Clearance Testing Recommendation

Per §9: Condition 2 — visual clearance acceptable. Recommend (but not mandatory) hygienist air sample for owner's documentation, given asthmatic tenant. Reinstatement condition: exhaust fan operational for minimum 7 days, confirmed functioning. New plasterboard, paint with anti-mould additive (Intergrain MDF or equivalent). Owner advised in writing: exhaust fan maintenance schedule (clean every 6 months).

You are an IICRC S770 certified fire and smoke damage restoration specialist producing scope-of-works documents for Australian insurance claims. Produce scopes in exactly 8 sections. Dollar amounts in AUD. Australian English.

Scope for a kitchen fire, protein smoke and dry smoke. 1980s brick veneer, 4br/2ba, 195 m². Origin: kitchen, electric cooktop fault. Smoke migration to open-plan living/dining and hallway. No suppression water. Char zone kitchen 14 m², smoke zone living/dining 38 m², odour zone bedrooms 82 m².

## 1. Fire Origin & Cause

**Confirmed origin:** Kitchen electric cooktop — heating element fault caused oil ignition in frying pan, then fire spread to overhead laminate cabinetry before self-extinguishment. QFES report reference: [to be inserted]. Electrical supply isolated by QFES at switchboard. No structural fire involvement beyond kitchen. **Smoke type: dual — protein smoke (cooking residue) and dry smoke (fast-burning laminate cabinetry, high-temperature).** IICRC S770 §4.1. Note: electrical inspection by licensed electrician required before power restored to kitchen circuit.

## 2. Smoke Migration Mapping by Zone

Per S770 §4.2:

| Zone | Rooms | Area | Visible Soot | Odour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Char | Kitchen | 14 m² | Heavy carbon + protein residue | Intense |
| Smoke | Living/Dining | 38 m² | Light dry soot on ceiling and upper walls | Strong |
| Odour | Bedrooms (4) + Hallway | 82 m² | No visible soot | Moderate-strong |

Migration pathway: kitchen ceiling → open-plan air volume → living/dining ceiling rose → hallway → bedroom door gaps. HVAC ducted system (ceiling grilles in all rooms): return grille in kitchen shows heavy soot contamination — duct system decontamination required.

## 3. Affected Materials by Zone

**Char (Kitchen 14 m²):** Overhead cabinetry (5 units, laminate particleboard) — total loss. Plasterboard ceiling and 2 × walls — remove. Ceramic tile splashback — clean in situ. Cooktop surround stainless steel — clean.
**Smoke (Living/Dining 38 m²):** Plasterboard ceiling — dry sponge + wet clean in situ. Plasterboard walls (upper) — clean. Carpet (polyester) — odour absorbed; cleaning assessment.
**Odour (Bedrooms 82 m²):** All surfaces — thermal fogging. Carpet — odour treatment.

## 4. Cleaning Methods by Material & Smoke Type

Per S770 §8. **Critical: dry sponge FIRST before any wet cleaning — protein and dry smoke both.**

1. Dry sponge: all sooted surfaces (ceiling, walls) — char and smoke zones
2. HEPA vacuum after dry sponge — capture loose soot
3. Protein smoke (cooktop surround, tiles, adjacent cabinets): enzyme-based cleaner (ZEP Enzyme or equivalent) → neutraliser rinse
4. Dry smoke (living/dining ceiling, walls): alkaline degreaser pH 10–11 → neutraliser rinse → dry
5. Char zone surfaces: cleaning not attempted — plasterboard removal
6. HVAC system: isolate. All supply and return grilles: remove and HEPA vacuum; wipe with antimicrobial. Duct interior survey: recommend HEPA vacuum treatment of accessible duct runs (HVAC contractor)

## 5. Contents Inventory — Restore vs Replace Assessment

Per S770 §9:
- Kitchen cabinets (5): total loss — structural damage
- Cooktop: replace — electrical fault origin
- Lounge suite (fabric 3-seater + 2): restore — hydroxyl chamber treatment + fabric cleaning
- Carpet living/dining: restore if cleaning removes odour — if odour persists post-hydroxyl, replace
- Bedding (4 × rooms): restore — commercial laundering with enzyme treatment
- HVAC ducted unit: functional assessment by HVAC contractor — cleaning scope, not replacement

## 6. Deodorisation Protocol

Per S770 §10:
- **Thermal fogging** (whole house): 2-hour treatment including roof space access hatch. Re-entry: 4-hour wait, 2-hour ventilation before occupants.
- **Hydroxyl generators** (2 × units): living/dining + master bedroom — 3 days continuous at 1 unit per 70 m²
- **Ozone:** NOT used (fabric contents present; hydroxyl preferred)
- HVAC: system sealed during fogging; filters replaced before restart

## 7. Structural Drying

No fire suppression water applied. No structural drying required. Monitor plasterboard replacement area for condensation during painting. Kitchen circuit electrical sign-off required from licensed electrician before power restored.

## 8. Clearance & Sign-Off

Per S770 §11:
- Visual inspection: no residual soot visible on any surface, all zones
- Odour panel: 3 technicians confirm smoke-odour-free in all rooms
- Air quality test: recommended for kitchen area given protein smoke (acrolein trace compounds)
- HVAC: operational clearance from HVAC contractor post-duct treatment
- Electrical: licensed electrician sign-off kitchen circuit
- Reinstatement trigger: kitchen cabinet replacement, ceiling/wall lining replacement, painting, carpet assessment

You are an Australian restoration specialist calculating IICRC S500:2025 equipment quantities for water damage jobs. For each request, provide: equipment list with quantities and justification, electrical load calculation per AS/NZS 3012:2019, and estimated drying duration. Dollar amounts in AUD.
Calculate equipment for: Category 2 Class 3, affected area 55 m², includes 3 wet wall panels (flood-cut). QLD ambient RH 72%, summer conditions.

## Equipment Calculation — Category 2 Class 3, 55 m²

### Equipment Quantities (IICRC S500:2025 ratios)

| Equipment | Calculation | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier | 55 m² ÷ 40 m² per unit = 1.375 → **2 units** | **2 × 100 L/day LGR** |
| Air Movers | 55 m² ÷ 15 m² per unit = 3.67 + 3 wet wall panels = 6.67 → **7 units** | **7 × axial 1.5 kW** |
| HEPA Air Scrubber | Mandatory Category 2 (§5.3). 55 m² ÷ 100 m² per unit = 0.55 → **1 unit** | **1 × HEPA 600 m³/h** |

### Placement Notes
- Air movers: 4 × floor-level at 45° to wet floor surfaces; 3 × angled through flood-cut wall openings (one per wet wall panel)
- Dehumidifiers: 1 × near floor moisture zone; 1 × elevated for ceiling air mass control
- Air scrubber: central position, exhaust directed toward makeshift exit (window or door)

### Electrical Load — AS/NZS 3012:2019

- 7 × air movers @ 1.5 kW = 10.5 kW
- 2 × LGR dehumidifiers @ 1.0 kW each = 2.0 kW
- 1 × air scrubber @ 0.7 kW = 0.7 kW
- **Total: 13.2 kW**

**Circuit distribution required:** 13.2 kW across 2 × 20A/240V circuits (max 4.8 kW each at 100% = 9.6 kW), or 2 × 32A circuits. Recommend 32A temporary distribution board from restoration company trailer. Load per 32A/240V circuit = 7.68 kW max (80% rule). Split: Circuit A: 4 air movers + 1 dehu = 7.0 kW (91% of 7.68 — borderline; use 32A circuit confirmed). Circuit B: 3 air movers + 1 dehu + scrubber = 6.7 kW (87% — use 32A). **Licensed electrician to confirm available supply before setup.**

### Estimated Drying Duration

- Class 3 baseline: 7–10 days
- Category 2 modifier: antimicrobial delays at Day 1 and Day 3 (+0 days on drying time — but required)
- QLD summer ambient RH 72%: high ambient RH increases drying time by estimated 20–30%
- **Estimated: 9–13 days**
- Escalation trigger: if Day 7 average moisture reading > 30% WME across monitoring points → assess desiccant dehumidification upgrade

You are an Australian restoration specialist calculating IICRC S500:2025 equipment quantities for water damage jobs. Provide equipment list, electrical load per AS/NZS 3012:2019, and drying estimate. Dollar amounts in AUD.
Calculate equipment for: Category 1 Class 2, single room carpet wet, 18 m². No wall cavity involvement. Standard residential GPOs available.

## Equipment Calculation — Category 1 Class 2, 18 m² (Carpet Only)

### Equipment Quantities (IICRC S500:2025 ratios)

| Equipment | Calculation | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier | 18 m² ÷ 40 m² = 0.45 → **1 unit minimum** | **1 × 70 L/day LGR** |
| Air Movers | 18 m² ÷ 15 m² = 1.2 → **2 units** (round up + account for Class 2 carpet lift) | **2 × axial 1.5 kW** |
| HEPA Air Scrubber | Not required — Category 1, no contamination | None |

**Carpet pad/underlay:** Remove and dispose if saturated (Category 1 pad retains moisture — drying in situ not recommended for carpet pad per S500:2025 §8). Carpet: dry in place using air mover 'tent' technique (raise carpet edge 150 mm off subfloor, direct air mover underneath).

### Electrical Load — AS/NZS 3012:2019

- 2 × air movers @ 1.5 kW = 3.0 kW
- 1 × LGR dehumidifier @ 0.8 kW = 0.8 kW
- **Total: 3.8 kW**

Standard residential 10A GPO rated 2.4 kW. **Use 15A circuit or dedicated 20A circuit.** Total draw 3.8 kW on 20A/240V circuit = 3.8 ÷ 4.8 kW = **79% — acceptable (≤80% per AS/NZS 3012:2019)**. Single circuit sufficient.

### Estimated Drying Duration

- Class 2 (carpet + subfloor moisture): 5–7 days
- Category 1 modifier: standard protocol, no antimicrobial delays
- **Estimated: 5 days** under normal QLD ambient conditions

Equipment calc for Class 4 drying. Heritage Queenslander hardwood flooring, 3 rooms, 62 m². Category 1. High ambient RH 76% (QLD summer). No carpet — hardwood T&G floor boards only.

## Equipment Calculation — Category 1 Class 4, 62 m² Hardwood Flooring

### Equipment Quantities (IICRC S500:2025 §7.5 — Class 4 specialty protocol)

| Equipment | Calculation | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier | 62 m² ÷ 40 m² = 1.55 → **2 units** | **2 × 100 L/day LGR** |
| Air Movers (centrifugal, low-profile) | Class 4 uplift: 62 m² ÷ 15 m² = 4.1 → 5 base; +2 for Class 4 cross-floor coverage = **7 units** | **7 × centrifugal low-profile 1.5 kW** |
| HEPA Air Scrubber | Not required — Cat 1 | None |
| Desiccant Dehumidifier (standby) | Standby only — deploy if Day 7 hardwood average > 30% WME in 76% RH ambient | Standby: 1 × 40 L/hr desiccant |

**Air mover type:** Centrifugal low-profile (Injectidry-style or AirPath system preferred) — flat floor placement, horizontal airflow across hardwood board surface grain. Axial units not suitable — insufficient floor-hugging airflow velocity for hardwood Class 4.

### Electrical Load — AS/NZS 3012:2019

- 7 × low-profile centrifugal @ 1.5 kW = 10.5 kW
- 2 × LGR @ 1.0 kW = 2.0 kW
- **Total: 12.5 kW**

Distribution: 2 × 20A circuits (max 4.8 kW each) insufficient. **Temporary 32A distribution board required.** 2 × 32A circuits @ 7.68 kW max (80%): Circuit A — 4 air movers + 1 dehu = 7.0 kW (91% — 32A circuit confirmed); Circuit B — 3 air movers + 1 dehu = 6.5 kW (85% — 32A confirmed). Licensed electrician to verify supply capacity for heritage property (likely older switchboard — check RCD protection and circuit rating before connection).

### Estimated Drying Duration

- Class 4 baseline: 14–21 days
- High ambient RH 76% modifier: +20–30% to drying time
- Desiccant escalation: deploy if Day 7 average >30% WME
- **Estimated: 18–28 days** under 76% RH ambient without desiccant upgrade
- **With desiccant upgrade (Day 7 if needed): 14–18 days**
- Cupping monitoring: weekly measurement, maximum acceptable 2 mm variance across board width

Equipment for Category 3 sewage backup, 35 m² affected. Ground floor slab, no subfloor. Living room and hallway.

## Equipment Calculation — Category 3, 35 m², Ground Floor Slab

### Equipment Quantities (IICRC S500:2025 §5.4 — Category 3 mandatory protocols)

| Equipment | Calculation | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier | 35 m² ÷ 40 m² = 0.88 → **1 unit** | **1 × 100 L/day LGR** |
| Air Movers | 35 m² ÷ 15 m² = 2.3 → **3 units** (post-material-removal, structure drying) | **3 × axial 1.5 kW** |
| HEPA Air Scrubber | **Mandatory** Category 3. Run continuously during active decontamination. 35 m² ÷ 100 m² = 0.35 → **1 unit minimum** | **1 × HEPA 800 m³/h** |

**Note:** All porous materials (carpet, underlay, plasterboard to flood-cut line) removed before air movers deployed — equipment runs on decontaminated bare slab only. HEPA scrubber operational BEFORE any removal work commences.

### Level B PPE — Mandatory Category 3 (§5.4)
Full-face respirator (P3/OV), impermeable Tyvek coveralls, double nitrile gloves, boot covers. Decontamination anteroom or bucket decon at site exit.

### Electrical Load — AS/NZS 3012:2019
- 3 × air movers @ 1.5 kW = 4.5 kW
- 1 × LGR @ 1.0 kW = 1.0 kW
- 1 × HEPA scrubber @ 0.7 kW = 0.7 kW
- **Total: 6.2 kW** — single 32A circuit compliant (6.2 ÷ 7.68 = 81%)

### Estimated Drying Duration
- Category 3, slab, post-full-porous-removal: **7–10 days**
- Slab drying target: ≤4% WME (§11.4)
- Antimicrobial applications Days 1, 2, 4 — does not extend drying time but must be completed before drying equipment placement each day
- Independent hygienist ATP clearance required before reinstatement — add 1–2 days to timeline

Equipment for Category 1 Class 3, 90 m² whole house event. 4 rooms + hallway, ceiling void saturated throughout. Plasterboard ceilings removed. Timber frame construction.

## Equipment Calculation — Category 1 Class 3, 90 m², Whole House

### Equipment Quantities

| Equipment | Calculation | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| LGR Dehumidifier | 90 m² ÷ 40 m² = 2.25 → **3 units** (Class 3 ceiling void = elevated moisture load) | **3 × 100 L/day LGR** |
| Air Movers | 90 m² ÷ 15 m² = 6 base + 8 wet wall cavities (2 per affected room) = **14 units** | **14 × axial 1.5 kW** |
| HEPA Air Scrubber | Category 1 — not mandatory. **Recommend 1 unit** given ceiling cavity debris release during plasterboard removal | **1 × HEPA 600 m³/h (recommended)** |

### Placement Strategy
- Dehumidifiers: 1 per end of house + 1 central — triangulated moisture extraction
- Air movers: 2 per room directed into flood-cut wall cavities; remaining units floor-level at 45° to structural framing
- Ceiling void: with plasterboard removed, direct 2 × air movers upward into exposed roof space for truss/batten drying

### Electrical Load — AS/NZS 3012:2019
- 14 × air movers @ 1.5 kW = 21.0 kW
- 3 × LGR @ 1.0 kW = 3.0 kW
- 1 × HEPA @ 0.7 kW = 0.7 kW
- **Total: 24.7 kW**

House supply typically 60A/240V = 14.4 kW max (80%). **External temporary generator or 3-phase distribution board required.** Restoration company 30 kVA trailer-mounted generator recommended. Distribute across 4 × 20A circuits: ≤4.8 kW each. Layout: 3–4 air movers per circuit + 1 dehu. Licensed electrician mandatory for this load level.

### Estimated Drying Duration
- Class 3 (ceiling/walls + structural framing): **10–14 days**
- Category 1: no antimicrobial delay
- Whole house: additional 2–3 days for deep timber framing
- **Estimated: 12–17 days**

You are an Australian restoration specialist interpreting moisture readings for IICRC S500:2025 drying validation. For each set of readings, provide: drying progress assessment, any concerning readings, and whether the drying goal has been achieved per IICRC S500:2025 §11.4. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Day 7 moisture readings for Category 2 Class 2 job. Plasterboard readings: 18%, 17%, 16%, 19%, 17%, 18%. Concrete slab: 5%, 4%, 4%, 6%, 5%. Timber subfloor (2 readings): 16%, 15%. RH: 52%. Target: plasterboard ≤17%, slab ≤4%, timber ≤16%. Is drying goal achieved?

## Day 7 Drying Assessment — Category 2 Class 2

### Reading Analysis per IICRC S500:2025 §11.4

**Plasterboard (target ≤17% WME):**
- Readings: 18%, 17%, 16%, 19%, 17%, 18%
- Compliant: 17%, 16%, 17% (3 readings ✓)
- **Non-compliant: 18%, 19%, 18% (3 readings above target)**
- Assessment: **NOT MET** — 3 of 6 readings exceed target. Average: 17.5% WME.

**Concrete Slab (target ≤4% WME):**
- Readings: 5%, 4%, 4%, 6%, 5%
- Compliant: 4%, 4% (2 readings ✓)
- **Non-compliant: 5%, 6%, 5%**
- Assessment: **NOT MET** — 3 of 5 readings above target. Average: 4.8% WME.

**Timber Subfloor (target ≤16% WME):**
- Readings: 16%, 15%
- Both compliant ✓
- Assessment: **MET**

**Psychrometric — RH 52%:** Marginal. Target ≤50% for drying validation. Currently 2 percentage points above target — equipment should remain operational.

### Drying Goal Status: **NOT ACHIEVED**

Drying goal cannot be certified at Day 7. Reasons:
1. Plasterboard: 3 of 6 readings above 17% WME threshold
2. Concrete slab: 3 of 5 readings above 4% WME threshold
3. RH at 52% — not yet below 50%

### Recommended Actions

1. **Continue equipment for minimum 48 hours** — do not demobilise
2. **Investigate non-compliant plasterboard points** (18–19% WME): check for concealed moisture behind skirting boards or within wall cavity at those points — may indicate undiscovered wet zone
3. **Slab at 5–6% WME**: concrete slab drying is slow — normal progression but not yet at target. Do not reduce dehumidifier capacity.
4. **RH 52%**: check dehumidifier drainage — if tank full or drain blocked, performance is impacted. Verify drain line clear.
5. **Next assessment: Day 9** — if all readings compliant on Day 9, Day 10 reading confirms 2 consecutive compliant days. Drying Goal Certificate may be issued Day 10.

**Do not schedule reinstatement trades** until Drying Goal Certificate issued.
You are an Australian restoration specialist interpreting moisture readings for IICRC S500:2025 drying validation. Dollar amounts in AUD.
Day 5 readings, Category 1 Class 1. Plasterboard only: 14%, 13%, 15%, 14%. RH: 47%. Target ≤17% WME. Is drying goal achieved?

## Day 5 Drying Assessment — Category 1 Class 1

### Reading Analysis per §11.4

**Plasterboard (target ≤17% WME):**
- All 4 readings: 14%, 13%, 15%, 14% — all compliant ✓
- Average: 14.0% WME

**RH: 47%** — below 50% threshold ✓

### Drying Goal Status: **ACHIEVED — CONDITIONALLY**

All plasterboard readings below target (17% WME). RH below 50%. Class 1 protocol requires 3 consecutive compliant daily readings. If Day 3 and Day 4 readings were also compliant (confirm in monitoring log), **Drying Goal Certificate can be issued today (Day 5)**.

**Issue certificate if:** Day 3 readings ≤17% WME AND Day 4 readings ≤17% WME — confirmed compliant on all 3 consecutive days (Day 3, 4, 5).

**If Day 3 or Day 4 readings were not all compliant:** Continue monitoring. Day 5 is first confirmed compliant reading — need Day 6 and Day 7 compliant readings to complete 3-consecutive-day requirement.

### Demobilisation

Assuming 3-day consecutive compliance confirmed: demobilise all equipment today. Drying Goal Certificate issued in RestoreAssist (§11.4). Notify insurer and builder — reinstatement can proceed. No residual moisture concerns at current readings.
You are an Australian restoration specialist interpreting moisture readings for IICRC S500:2025 drying validation. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Day 10 readings, Category 1 Class 3 job, hardwood flooring. 12 grid points: 18%, 17%, 16%, 19%, 20%, 17%, 15%, 16%, 18%, 17%, 19%, 18%. Target ≤14% WME (QLD hardwood EMC per §11.4 climate-adjusted). RH 58%. How concerned should I be?

## Day 10 Assessment — Category 1 Class 4, Hardwood Flooring

### Reading Analysis per §11.4 (QLD Climate-Adjusted Target ≤14% WME)

**12-point grid readings:** 18%, 17%, 16%, 19%, 20%, 17%, 15%, 16%, 18%, 17%, 19%, 18%

- Compliant (≤14%): **NONE** — 0 of 12 readings at or below target
- Range: 15%–20% WME
- Average: **17.4% WME**
- Distance from target: average 3.4% above target — not trivial

**RH: 58%** — significantly above 50% threshold. Elevated ambient RH is the primary concern.

### Assessment: HIGH CONCERN — Well Above Target at Day 10

For Class 4 hardwood (§7.5), Day 10 at 17.4% average WME against a 14% target is a significant lag. However, hardwood drying curves are non-linear — the final 3–5% WME is the slowest phase.

**Concerns:**
1. **RH 58% is the primary problem.** Hardwood will not dry below 14% WME while ambient RH is 58% — the equilibrium moisture content of hardwood at 58% RH is approximately 10–12% WME, which means drying is theoretically possible, but the rate will be extremely slow at this RH. Dehumidifier performance must be investigated.
2. Check: Is dehumidifier drain line clear? Is tank empty? Any refrigerant leak? At Day 10, LGR units should be achieving RH well below 50% in a closed environment.
3. **Desiccant dehumidification escalation triggered:** Day 7 threshold was >30% WME — already passed. At Day 10 with RH 58%, **desiccant dehumidifier deployment is now strongly recommended.** Desiccant units are effective down to 15% RH and will significantly accelerate final hardwood drying.

**Estimated revised timeline:** With desiccant upgrade and RH brought to 35–40%, hardwood at 17–20% WME should reach ≤14% within 7–12 additional days. **Revised estimate: Day 17–22 for drying goal achievement.** Report delay to insurer with desiccant upgrade justification (S500:2025 §7.5 Class 4 protocol reference).
You are an Australian restoration specialist interpreting moisture readings for IICRC S500:2025 drying validation. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Day 8 readings, Category 3 Class 2. Concrete slab 6 points: 3%, 3%, 4%, 3%, 4%, 3%. Plasterboard remnants 4 points: 14%, 15%, 14%, 16%. Timber framing 4 points: 15%, 14%, 16%, 15%. RH: 48%. All targets: slab ≤4%, plasterboard ≤17%, timber ≤16%.

## Day 8 Assessment — Category 3 Class 2

### Reading Analysis per §11.4

**Concrete Slab (target ≤4% WME):**
- All 6 readings: 3%, 3%, 4%, 3%, 4%, 3% — all compliant ✓
- Average: 3.3% WME — well within target

**Plasterboard (target ≤17% WME):**
- All 4 readings: 14%, 15%, 14%, 16% — all compliant ✓
- Average: 14.75% WME

**Timber Framing (target ≤16% WME):**
- All 4 readings: 15%, 14%, 16%, 15% — all compliant ✓
- Average: 15.0% WME

**RH: 48%** — below 50% ✓

### Drying Goal Status: **CONDITIONALLY ACHIEVED**

All 14 reading points compliant. RH below 50%. This is the first fully compliant day — need Day 9 and Day 10 readings to confirm 3 consecutive compliant readings before issuing certificate.

**Action:** Continue equipment operation. Do not demobilise. Schedule Day 9 and Day 10 monitoring.

**Category 3 additional requirement:** Independent hygienist ATP clearance test must be completed in addition to moisture readings. ATP swab test should be scheduled now — hygienist booking should be made for Day 10 or Day 11. Drying Goal Certificate and ATP clearance certificate both required before reinstatement. Notify insurer and builder of expected Day 10–11 completion.
You are an Australian restoration specialist interpreting moisture readings for IICRC S500:2025 drying validation. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Unexpected reading spike on Day 6. Category 1 Class 2 job, seemed to be drying well. Day 5 plasterboard average was 19% WME. Day 6 readings: 24%, 26%, 22%, 25%, 23%, 24%. Dehumidifier appears to be working. RH jumped from 52% to 74%. What happened?

## Day 6 Spike Analysis — Unexpected Moisture Increase

### Immediate Assessment

A jump from 19% WME average (Day 5) to 24% average (Day 6) with concurrent RH increase from 52% to 74% is a **significant event requiring urgent investigation** before continuing with standard drying protocol.

### Most Probable Causes (in order of likelihood)

**1. New or ongoing water source (most likely — investigate first)**
- Check: Is there a new or continuing water source not identified in the initial assessment?
- Action: Walk the site for new wet areas, check under cabinets, behind appliances, inside wall cavities at flood-cut opening. Re-probe all original moisture points AND check areas not previously wet.
- Rationale: Day 5 to Day 6 increase with simultaneous RH spike is the classic pattern for a secondary source being discovered, or a source not fully isolated.

**2. Dehumidifier failure**
- Device may appear operational but not extracting moisture — check actual water collected in last 24 hours vs expected rate. 70 L/day unit should yield 3–4 L per hour in a 52% RH environment. If tank has <10 L, unit is not working correctly.
- Check: refrigerant pressure, coil frost (over-icing), airflow through coils.

**3. Doors or windows opened (containment breach)**
- Someone may have opened windows or doors overnight, flooding the space with 74% RH outdoor air. RH 74% ambient is consistent with QLD summer outdoor conditions — check with owner/occupant.

**4. Hidden wet zone released**
- Wall cavity or ceiling void moisture reaching equilibrium with room air as it warms — delayed moisture migration. Less common in Day 5→6 window but possible in Class 3 events.

### Immediate Actions

1. **Identify and re-isolate any water source today** — do not wait for Day 7 monitoring
2. **Verify dehumidifier water extraction** — document L collected since last visit
3. **Confirm site has been closed** — windows/doors shut since Day 5
4. **Extend monitoring to twice-daily** until spike resolved
5. **Do not proceed toward drying goal validation** until readings return to downward trend and cause is understood
6. Document spike with photographs and full reading set — report to insurer if source is new water event (separate loss event potentially)

You are an Australian restoration specialist. Classify the following job description into the most likely IICRC claim type, water category (if applicable), estimated damage class, and recommended scope action. Claim types: water_damage, mould_remediation, fire_smoke, storm, biohazard, contents. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Job description: 'Called out to a unit in Fortitude Valley. Tenant reported a smell and discoloured water coming up through the bathroom floor. Drain has been gurgling for 3 days. Strong sewage odour throughout. Light brown water staining on lower bathroom walls. Tenant visibly unwell.'

## Claim Classification

**Primary claim type: water_damage**

**IICRC Water Category: Category 3 (Black Water)**
Basis: Sewage odour, discoloured water (brown), drain gurgling for 3 days before backflow, tenant symptomatic illness. Per IICRC S500:2025 §5.4 — grossly contaminated water. Do not downgrade to Category 2 pending investigation; treat as Category 3 until proven otherwise.

**Estimated Damage Class: Class 2**
Basis: Bathroom affected, limited spread described. Wall staining to lower section (likely <600 mm) suggests wicking but no multi-room spread reported. Upgrade to Class 3 if water found in wall cavities during inspection.

**Recommended Immediate Actions:**
1. **Occupant health advisory — immediate.** Tenant reports illness — issue written health advisory, advise vacate until remediation complete. Notify building manager.
2. **Level B PPE before entry** — full-face respirator P3, Tyvek coveralls, double nitrile gloves
3. **Source investigation:** Contact Body Corporate — likely council sewer main issue or blocked shared drain. Do not commence extraction until source is confirmed isolated.
4. **Notify insurer** before non-emergency works — body corporate and private insurer notification required
5. **All porous materials in Cat 3 contact must be removed** — no exceptions per §5.4

**Estimated value range:** $4,000–$9,000 AUD (single bathroom Cat 3, standard unit) depending on extent of structural involvement found on opening.

You are an Australian restoration specialist. Classify the following job description into the most likely IICRC claim type, water category (if applicable), estimated damage class, and recommended scope action. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Job: 'Property owner called to say ceiling in lounge room sagging after last night's storm. Visible water staining on plasterboard, one section buckled about 800 mm diameter. No active leak — storm has passed. Roof inspection pending. Walls dry. No carpet.'

## Claim Classification

**Primary claim type: storm**

**IICRC Water Category: Category 1 (Clean Water — rainwater)**
Basis: Storm-driven rainwater intrusion. No contamination source. Category 1 classification applies unless roof space inspection reveals animal contamination, chemical deposits, or the water has been static in the ceiling void for >72 hours (Category 1→2 upgrade risk if undetected for extended period — confirm loss date with owner).

**Estimated Damage Class: Class 1–2**
Basis: Ceiling moisture apparent, walls dry, no carpet or floor moisture. Likely Class 1 (partial room) unless ceiling void inspection reveals extensive saturation — inspect for insulation saturation and sarking condition before finalising class.

**Recommended Immediate Actions:**
1. **Make-safe assessment:** Sagging plasterboard — structural risk. If sagging >50 mm, consider temporary prop or controlled removal before debris falls. Photograph before any intervention.
2. **Roof inspection:** Confirm breach point. ICA make-safe obligation — tarpaulin must be installed within 24–48 hours if roof is exposed.
3. **Ceiling void probe:** Drill 3 × inspection holes at sagging centre and 500 mm radius — confirm water volume and insulation saturation
4. **Moisture survey:** Map all affected ceiling surfaces; check top of wall plates for wicking
5. **Do not replace plasterboard until drying goal achieved** — premature reinstatement traps moisture

**Estimated value range:** $2,500–$6,500 AUD (single-room ceiling replacement + drying + roof make-safe + tile repair).

You are an Australian restoration specialist. Classify the following job description into the most likely IICRC claim type, water category (if applicable), estimated damage class, and recommended scope action. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Job: 'Tenant complained of black spots on walls in 2 bedrooms. Property manager did an inspection and found black mould on lower walls of both rooms. Approximate area 0.8 m² total. No visible water damage. No exhaust fans installed in either room. Property built 1985.'

## Claim Classification

**Primary claim type: mould_remediation**

**IICRC S520 Contamination Condition: Condition 2**
Basis: Visible growth 0.8 m² total across two rooms. Condition 2 threshold is <1 m² isolated growth (§4.2). However, note: 0.8 m² visible does not preclude Condition 3 being present behind walls — moisture source investigation (§5.1) and potential probe is required before committing to Condition 2 classification.

**Water Category:** Not applicable (mould — no water intrusion event).

**Moisture Source Indicators:**
- No exhaust fans in 1985-built bedrooms — condensation-driven growth highly likely
- 1985 construction: single-glazed aluminium windows probable — cold bridge condensation in QLD winter
- Lower wall growth pattern consistent with rising damp or condensation pooling at floor junction

**Recommended Actions:**
1. **Moisture investigation first (§5.1):** Moisture meter readings at affected walls. If >30% WME in plasterboard behind visible growth → wall cavity probe required → may upgrade to Condition 3.
2. **Pre-1990 construction note:** Check for asbestos-containing materials — 1985 textured ceilings, cornice, wall lining, and vinyl tiles may contain asbestos. **Do not disturb any suspect materials without asbestos testing first.** Engage licensed asbestos assessor if ACM suspected.
3. Source: Recommend landlord install exhaust fans in both bedrooms before or concurrent with remediation — without source resolution, remediation is ineffective per §5.1.
4. Contamination boundary: 0.8 m² visible + 500 mm perimeter per §6.3 → estimated 3.0–4.0 m² plasterboard for removal.
5. **Estimated value: $2,000–$3,500 AUD** (Condition 2, two rooms, 0.8 m² visible, with asbestos clearance contingency).

You are an Australian restoration specialist. Classify the following job description into the most likely IICRC claim type, water category (if applicable), estimated damage class, and recommended scope action. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Job: 'Insurance assessor referred job. Large Queenslander in Paddington. Smoke damage throughout from kitchen fire. Fire brigade confirmed electrical fault in old oven. Approx 30% of dwelling affected by smoke. No suppression water — brigade only attended briefly. Owner distraught — mentions heritage overlay and original pressed metal ceilings.'

## Claim Classification

**Primary claim type: fire_smoke**

**IICRC S770 Smoke Type:** Likely dry smoke (electrical fire) with possible protein smoke component (kitchen, oven). Electrical fires produce acrid, dry smoke residue. Confirm during initial inspection.

**Heritage Complexity — CRITICAL FLAGS:**
1. **Heritage overlay (Paddington QLD):** Brisbane City Council heritage overlay likely. Any works to external fabric or interior heritage elements require heritage architect involvement. Pressed metal ceilings classified as heritage fabric — cannot be replaced with modern materials without council approval. **Notify insurer immediately of heritage complication.**
2. **Pressed metal ceilings:** Non-standard cleaning challenge — HEPA vacuum and dry sponge only on pressed metal (no wet cleaning that may cause rusting). Specialist metal conservator may be required. Do not wet-clean pressed metal without specialist assessment.
3. **Pre-1900s Queenslander:** High probability of asbestos in fibrous plaster wall linings, textured ceilings, under-floor boards, and pipe lagging. **Mandatory asbestos survey before any works** — engage licensed asbestos assessor before restoration commencement.

**Smoke Zone Mapping:**
- Char zone: kitchen origin (to be mapped on inspection)
- Smoke zone: 30% of dwelling (~estimated 60–80 m² in a Paddington Queenslander)
- Odour zone: remainder of dwelling

**Recommended Immediate Actions:**
1. Commission asbestos survey — do not disturb any materials pending clearance
2. Engage heritage architect for written advice on pressed metal ceiling treatment
3. Notify Brisbane City Council heritage officer (or check if notification trigger applies)
4. Scope in two parts: restoration (smoke cleaning, deodorisation) and reinstatement (heritage-compliant)
5. **Estimated value range: $35,000–$90,000+ AUD** — heritage complexity, large Queenslander, specialist trades, potential asbestos management plan. Notify insurer that standard pricing does not apply.

You are an Australian restoration specialist. Classify the following job description into the most likely IICRC claim type, water category (if applicable), estimated damage class, and recommended scope action. Dollar amounts in AUD.

Job notes: 'Attended Collingwood Park address. Flexi-hose under bathroom vanity burst overnight. Property unoccupied — owners away for 5 days. Water has spread from bathroom through hallway into living room. Carpet fully saturated living room 24 m², hallway 10 m², bathroom 6 m². Water discoloured — slight yellowish tint. Standing water present.'

## Claim Classification

**Primary claim type: water_damage**

**IICRC Water Category: UPGRADE TO Category 2 — do not classify as Category 1**
Basis per S500:2025 §4.3: Category 1 water source (flexi-hose, clean water) BUT sitting for approximately 5 days (120 hours) in an unoccupied property. Category 1 water sitting >72 hours becomes Category 2 due to microbial amplification in warm, stagnant conditions. Discolouration (yellowish tint) and probable elevated bacterial count confirms Category 2 classification. **Do not document as Category 1 — this exposes both the operator and insurer to risk if mould is later discovered.** Category 2 protocols mandatory: antimicrobial treatment, carpet and underlay disposal, PPE minimum Level C.

**Estimated Damage Class: Class 3**
Basis: Multi-room spread (bathroom 6 m² + hallway 10 m² + living 24 m² = 40 m²), carpet fully saturated, 5-day dwell time likely means moisture has wicked up walls to 300–600 mm. Probe wall cavities before finalising — Class 4 possible if subfloor also saturated.

**Critical Actions:**
1. **Category upgrade documentation:** Note in job record: 'Category 1 source, elevated to Category 2 per S500:2025 §4.3 — unattended >72 hours, water discoloured.'
2. **Carpet and underlay removal required** — Category 2 saturated porous contents disposal
3. **Standing water extraction first** — wet vacuum or truck-mount before any moisture readings
4. **Wall cavity probe** after extraction — moisture mapping at 200 mm, 400 mm, 600 mm heights on all perimeter walls
5. **Antimicrobial treatment** to all exposed slab and framing surfaces after material removal
6. **Notify insurer of Category 2 classification** — scope and cost implications above standard flexi-hose water event

**Estimated value range: $5,500–$12,000 AUD** (40 m² Cat 2 Class 3, carpet disposal, extended drying period, antimicrobial treatment).
